title: Stanford Type A Aortic Dissection
chief_complaint: Severe tearing chest pain radiating to my back. Worst pain of my life - started suddenly while I was watching TV. I have high blood pressure.

physical_exam.Cardiac: Diastolic murmur of aortic regurgitation (new). BP differential: Right arm 180/100, Left arm 140/80 (20% difference).
physical_exam.Pulses: Right radial pulse diminished compared to left. Femoral pulses weak bilaterally.

hidden_data.cxr: Widened mediastinum (>8cm). Obliteration of aortic knob. Tracheal deviation to right.
hidden_data.ct_chest: Stanford Type A aortic dissection. Intimal flap in ascending aorta extending from aortic root to descending aorta. Involves brachiocephalic artery. Pericardial effusion present (hemopericardium - impending tamponade).
hidden_data.echo: TEE confirms dissection flap in ascending aorta. Severe aortic regurgitation. Moderate pericardial effusion with early tamponade physiology.

correct_diagnosis: Stanford Type A Acute Aortic Dissection with Aortic Regurgitation, Malperfusion (Right Arm), and Impending Tamponade
key_learning_points: Stanford A = ascending aorta involved (regardless of origin). SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Stanford B = descending aorta only → medical management
Type A mortality increases 1-2% per hour. 50% die within 48 hours without surgery. Emergency cardiothoracic surgery consult
Physical exam: Pulse deficit (>20mmHg difference between arms), aortic regurgitation murmur, neurologic deficits (carotid dissection). Widened mediastinum on CXR
CT with contrast is diagnostic gold standard. TEE if patient unstable for CT. BP control: Target SBP 100-120 with Beta-blockers FIRST (Esmolol) then vasodilators (Nicardipine). Avoid anticoagulants
